# Fiberlytic Field Map — Developer Handoff Package

**Prepared for**: rebuilding or integrating the Field Map module into another application.
**Scope**: the interactive map at route `/kmz/:projectId` (`src/pages/KmzMap.tsx`) and everything it depends on — drawing/redlining, Work Objects, photos, billing, production/P&L linkage, PDF georeferencing, and the adjacent Map Cuts tool.
**Status of this document**: generated by direct source-code inspection (file/line citations throughout) on 2026-07-03. This is a **documentation-only** artifact — nothing in the running app was changed to produce it.

> **Read this first — two corrections to what you may have been told about this codebase:**
>
> 1. **The repo's own `CLAUDE.md` is out of date.** It describes a "PDF Print Reader + KMZ Builder" feature with pages `PrintReader.tsx`/`PrintReview.tsx` and a Mapbox-GL review screen (`MapView.tsx`, `ObjectEditorDrawer.tsx`). **These four files were deleted on 2026-07-01** (commit `b6b3c9c`, *"Delete legacy Print Reader/Review pages, superseded by GeoreferencePanel OCR"*). Their OCR/detection responsibilities were absorbed into `GeoreferencePanel.tsx`'s "detect" step (Leaflet-based) and a newer, separate `MapReading.tsx` page. Section 10 and Section 9 below describe what's actually live today, and flag the leftover dead code so you don't waste time integrating it.
> 2. **There are two, unrelated "identity" systems** in this app — don't conflate them: `AuthContext.tsx` wraps **real Firebase Authentication** (email/password, gates the whole app). `RoleContext.tsx` is a **separate, purely client-side "admin vs. field view" UI toggle** stored in `localStorage`, unrelated to Firebase, with zero server-side enforcement. See Section 24.

## 1. Full Architecture Overview

Fiberlytic is a **frontend-only** React + TypeScript single-page app — there is no server or database. All application data lives in the browser as one JSON object (`AppData`) persisted to `localStorage`, with large binary blobs (photos, PDF renders) kept in IndexedDB. The Field Map is one page within this larger app, but it is the single largest and most complex feature in the codebase.

### Where it lives

| File | Lines | Role |
|---|---|---|
| `src/pages/KmzMap.tsx` | **2,625** | The Field Map page itself. Leaflet map instance, every draw tool, panel wiring, mobile nav. The largest file in the repo. |
| `src/pages/KmzProduction.tsx` | — | Project-picker landing page; also owns KMZ **import** (see §10) |
| `src/pages/PdfPrintMode.tsx` | — | Sibling page: same markup data rendered onto a static PDF page instead of Leaflet, at `/kmz/:projectId/print/:fileId` |
| `src/pages/MapCuts.tsx` | — | Separate tool: slices one oversized PDF sheet into field-print-ready pages (§20). Shares no code with the Field Map. |
| `src/pages/MapReading.tsx` | — | Separate, newer OCR/tracing tool for cut-map pages. Not covered in depth here — out of scope, but mentioned where it clarifies what replaced the deleted Print Reader. |

### How the map is built

The map is **raw Leaflet** (`leaflet@1.9.4`), not `react-leaflet`. It's created imperatively in a `useEffect` and stored in a `ref`, not React state:

```ts
// src/pages/KmzMap.tsx (initialization, abridged)
const mapRef = useRef<L.Map | null>(null)
useEffect(() => {
  if (!mapContainerRef.current || mapRef.current) return
  const map = L.map(mapContainerRef.current, { center: [39.5, -98.35], zoom: 4, zoomControl: true })
  map.createPane('markups')
  map.getPane('markups')!.style.zIndex = '450'
  mapRef.current = map
  markupGroupRef.current = L.layerGroup().addTo(map)
  setMapReady(true)
  return () => { map.remove(); mapRef.current = null; setMapReady(false) }
}, [])
```

Base tiles are swapped reactively between two **free, keyless** tile sources — OpenStreetMap (street) and Esri World Imagery (satellite) — no Mapbox token is used anywhere in the live Field Map:

```ts
tileLayerRef.current = L.tileLayer(
  isSat
    ? 'https://server.arcgisonline.com/ArcGIS/rest/services/World_Imagery/MapServer/tile/{z}/{y}/{x}'
    : 'https://{s}.tile.openstreetmap.org/{z}/{x}/{y}.png',
  { attribution: isSat ? '© Esri' : '© OpenStreetMap contributors', maxZoom: 19 },
).addTo(map)
```

Pane z-order: KMZ vector features (Leaflet default pane, z=400) → hand-drawn markups (custom `markups` pane, z=450) → point markers (z=600).

### State shape (KmzMap.tsx)

Roughly 47 `useState` calls plus ~20 `useRef`s, organized into clear blocks:

- **Map/list state**: `mapReady`, `selectedFeature`, `search`, `listOpen`, `collapsedLayers`, `mapLayer` (`'street'|'satellite'`)
- **Draw-tool state**: `activeTool` (a `DrawTool | MarkupTool` union), `activeSubtype`, `rlColor`/`rlWeight`/`rlOpacity`, `rlLayer`, `visibleLayers`, `snapEnabled`
- **Selection/panels**: `selectedMarkup` (drives either `MarkupPanel` or `WorkObjectPropertiesPanel`), `quickActionsAnchor`, `editMode` (`'none'|'vertices'|'move'`)
- **Add Work wizard**: `addWorkModalOpen`, `addWorkMarkupId`
- **Mobile nav**: `mobileNav: 'map' | 'markers' | 'forms' | 'billing' | 'settings'`
- **Refs (imperative, non-React-rendered)**: Leaflet layer/group refs, in-progress draw-point buffers, the undo/redo stacks, a session-only callout position map (`calloutStateRef`)

Delete confirmation is delegated to a shared hook rather than inline state:
```ts
const deleteFlow = useMarkupDeleteFlow(softDeleteMarkup, activeEmployeeId)
```
defined in `src/lib/markupDelete.ts` — shared between Field Map and PDF Print Mode so the confirm/invoice-block rule can't drift between the two.

## 2. Frontend Components

Covered exhaustively in the file table above. The three you'll spend the most time in when rebuilding are:

- **`KmzMap.tsx`** — the map shell, all draw-tool interaction, panel orchestration.
- **`MarkupPanel.tsx`** (1,111 lines) — the full editing sidebar. Six tabs: `notes` (labeled "Details" in the UI), `photos`, `billing`, `inspection`, `history`, `attachments`. See §17 for a tab-by-tab breakdown.
- **`AddWorkModal.tsx`** (587 lines) — the guided creation wizard: Type → Draw → Details → Photos → Billing → Save. See §15 for its validation logic.

A fourth, smaller but important component: **`WorkObjectPropertiesPanel.tsx`** — a compact, draggable floating popup (Work Type / Crew / Quantity / Status only) shown *instead of* the big `MarkupPanel` sidebar whenever the selected markup has a `workObjectType`, so the map stays visible while doing quick edits. It writes through `updateMarkup()` immediately on every field change (no dirty/Save gate, unlike `MarkupPanel`'s Details tab).

Shared UI primitives used throughout (from the wider app, not Field-Map-specific): `Modal`, `Field`/`Input`/`Select`/`Textarea`/`Button` (`src/components/ui/Form.tsx`), `Card`/`CardHeader`/`CardBody`.

---

## 3. Backend / API Logic

**There is none.** This is the single most important architectural fact for anyone integrating this feature: Fiberlytic has no server, no REST/GraphQL API, and no database beyond the browser's own storage. Every "backend-shaped" concept in this codebase is actually client-side:

| What you might expect on a server | What actually exists here |
|---|---|
| A database | `AppData` — one big JSON object in `localStorage['fiberlytic:data:v1']` |
| A file/blob store | IndexedDB, via `src/lib/fileStore.ts` |
| An API layer | `src/store/DataContext.tsx` — a React Context exposing CRUD functions that mutate the in-memory `AppData` and re-persist it |
| Authentication | **Real** — Firebase Authentication (`src/store/AuthContext.tsx`), see §24 |
| Authorization / roles | **Fake** — a client-only `localStorage` toggle (`src/store/RoleContext.tsx`), see §24 |
| A sync/replication layer | A **placeholder** (`src/lib/syncQueue.ts`) that queues mutations locally and marks them "synced" the instant the browser fires its `online` event — no network call ever happens, see §24 |

If you are rebuilding this feature with a real backend, the natural seam is `DataContext.tsx`'s CRUD method signatures (§4) — swap their `setData(...)` bodies for real API calls and the rest of the app (all the components above) needs no changes, since every component reads/writes exclusively through the `useData()` hook.

---

## 4. Database Tables and Data Models

All types live in `src/types.ts`. There is no ORM/schema — these are plain TypeScript interfaces, and the "table" is simply an array field on the top-level `AppData` object.

### Core entity: `FieldMarkup`

Every hand-drawn shape, feature-drop pin, callout, or Work Object is one `FieldMarkup` record.

```ts
export interface FieldMarkup {
  id: string
  projectId: string

  // Tool and visual style
  tool: MarkupTool
  subtype?: string
  workObjectType?: WorkObjectTypeId
  color: string
  weight: number
  fillColor: string | null
  fillOpacity: number
  opacity: number

  // Geometry
  geometry: MarkupGeometry
  coordSpace?: 'latlng' | 'pdfPage'
  sourceProjectFileId?: string | null
  pageIndex?: number
  capturedLat?: number | null
  capturedLng?: number | null
  gpsUnavailableConfirmed?: boolean

  workId?: string | null

  // Content
  label: string | null
  fontSize: number
  fontFamily?: string
  fontBold?: boolean
  fontItalic?: boolean
  fontUnderline?: boolean
  fontStrikethrough?: boolean

  // Feature metadata (for feature-drop tools)
  featureType: string | null
  featureName: string | null
  notes: string | null

  // Measurements
  lengthFt: number | null
  quantity: number | null

  colorCode?: string

  // Smart construction markup fields
  workType?: 'underground' | 'aerial' | 'splicing' | 'general'
  assetType?: string
  assetCategory?: string
  size?: string
  material?: string
  unit?: string
  costCode?: string
  billingCode?: string
  isBillable?: boolean
  isProductionItem?: boolean
  isQCRequired?: boolean

  // Workflow
  status: MarkupStatus
  layer: MarkupLayer
  crewId: string | null

  lineStyle?: 'solid' | 'dashed' | 'dotted'
  /** Offline sync state — inert placeholder until a real sync target exists. */
  syncStatus?: 'local' | 'pending' | 'synced' | 'error'

  // Audit
  createdBy: string | null
  createdAt: string          // ISO datetime
  updatedAt: string | null
  lockedAt: string | null
  deletedAt?: string | null
  deletedBy?: string | null
}
```

```ts
export interface MarkupGeometry {
  latlngs?: [number, number][]                    // pen, line, arrow, polygon, polyline
  bounds?:  [[number, number], [number, number]]  // rect
  center?:  [number, number]                      // circle, text, callout, feature drops
  radius?:  number                                // circle (meters)
}
```

### Related entities

```ts
export interface MarkupBilling {
  id: string
  markupId: string
  date?: string | null
  crewId?: string | null
  rateCode: string
  description: string
  unitType: string
  quantity: number
  rate: number
  total: number             // quantity × rate
  billable: boolean
  invoiceStatus: 'not_billed' | 'invoiced' | 'approved' | 'paid'
  notes: string | null
  invoiceId?: string | null
}

export interface MarkupPhoto {
  id: string
  markupId: string
  caption: string | null
  takenAt: string
  uploadedBy: string | null
  lat: number | null
  lng: number | null
  phase?: PhotoProofType | null   // which required-photo phase this satisfies
}

export interface MarkupVideo { id: string; markupId: string; caption: string | null; takenAt: string }

export type InspectionResult = 'pass' | 'fail' | 'na'
export interface InspectionItem { id: string; label: string; result: InspectionResult; notes: string | null }
export interface MarkupInspection {
  id: string; markupId: string; items: InspectionItem[]
  overallResult: 'pass' | 'fail' | 'pending'; notes: string | null
  createdBy: string | null; createdAt: string
}

export interface MarkupAttachment { id: string; markupId: string; fileName: string; mimeType: string; uploadedAt: string }

export type MarkupHistoryAction =
  | 'created' | 'field_changed' | 'photo_added' | 'photo_removed'
  | 'billing_added' | 'billing_removed' | 'inspection_added'
  | 'locked' | 'unlocked' | 'deleted'
export interface MarkupHistoryEntry {
  id: string; markupId: string; timestamp: string; actor: string | null
  action: MarkupHistoryAction; field?: string; oldValue?: string | null; newValue?: string | null
}

export interface GeoreferenceControlPoint { px: { x: number; y: number }; lat: number; lng: number }
export interface GeoreferencedOverlay {
  id: string; projectId: string; sourceProjectFileId?: string | null
  imageBlobKey: string; pageIndex: number
  naturalWidth: number; naturalHeight: number
  controlPoints: GeoreferenceControlPoint[]
  opacity: number; visible: boolean; createdAt: string
}

export interface AerialPole {
  poleNumber: number; lat: number; lng: number
  tickMark: string | null; notes: string | null
  crewName: string | null; dateTime: string | null; completed: boolean
}
export interface AerialLashFiberRun {
  id: string; projectId: string; status: 'in_progress' | 'complete'
  poles: AerialPole[]; notes: string | null
  totalFootage: number; totalPoles: number
  color: string; colorCode: string; createdAt: string; updatedAt: string | null
}
```

### Enums / lookup tables

```ts
export type MarkupStatus = 'pending' | 'in_progress' | 'complete' | 'qc_needed' | 'rejected' | 'approved' | 'billed'
export type MarkupLayer  = 'crew' | 'supervisor' | 'qc' | 'as_built' | 'production' | 'billing'

export const MARKUP_STATUS_META: Record<MarkupStatus, { label: string; color: string }> = {
  pending:     { label: 'Pending',     color: '#6b7280' },
  in_progress: { label: 'In Progress', color: '#f59e0b' },
  complete:    { label: 'Complete',    color: '#22c55e' },
  qc_needed:   { label: 'QC Needed',   color: '#a855f7' },
  rejected:    { label: 'Rejected',    color: '#ef4444' },
  approved:    { label: 'Approved',    color: '#06b6d4' },
  billed:      { label: 'Billed',      color: '#10b981' },
}
```

`MarkupTool` is a ~90-value union covering four families: freeform drawing primitives (`pen|line|rect|circle|polygon|text|callout|...`), generic feature-drop pins (`handhole|bore|splice_point|pole|...`), circular structure markers (`struct_s|struct_hh|...`), and ~70 "engineering symbols" grouped by work discipline (Directional Drill, Aerial Strand, Handhole/Vault, Distribution/Feeder Fiber, Drop, Pole, Anchor/Down Guy, Splicing, Trenching, Plowing, Sub-Ducting). Full list is in `src/types.ts:701-747`.

`WorkObjectTypeId` (22 values) and `PhotoProofType` (the required-photo-phase enum) are covered in §12/§15.

### Production/P&L entities (the destination of Field Map billing — full detail in §22)

```ts
export interface ProductionEntry {
  id: string; date: string; projectId: string; crewId: string
  footage: number; hours: number; notes?: string
  equipmentIds?: string[]
  sourceMarkupId?: string   // FK → FieldMarkup.id, set when auto-generated from billing
}
export interface ProductionLineItem {
  id: string; productionEntryId: string
  unitCode: string; description: string; uom: string
  quantity: number
  rateSnapshot: number       // frozen at entry time, never recalculated
  extendedTotal: number      // quantity × rateSnapshot
  sourceMarkupBillingId?: string   // FK → MarkupBilling.id, lets later edits find this record
}
export interface PnLEntry {
  id: string; date: string; projectId: string
  revenue: number; laborCost: number; materialCost: number; equipmentCost: number; otherCost: number
  productionEntryId?: string   // FK → ProductionEntry.id
}
```

### The relevant `AppData` array fields

```ts
fieldMarkups: FieldMarkup[]
markupPhotos: MarkupPhoto[]
markupBilling: MarkupBilling[]
markupVideos: MarkupVideo[]
markupInspections: MarkupInspection[]
markupAttachments: MarkupAttachment[]
markupHistory: MarkupHistoryEntry[]
aerialLashFiberRuns: AerialLashFiberRun[]
fieldMapOverlays: GeoreferencedOverlay[]
favoriteUnitCodes: string[]
production: ProductionEntry[]
productionLineItems: ProductionLineItem[]
pnl: PnLEntry[]
```

## 5. Dependencies and Libraries

### Runtime dependencies used by the Field Map

| Package | Version | Used for |
|---|---|---|
| `leaflet` | `^1.9.4` | The map itself — raw API, no react-leaflet |
| `@types/leaflet` | `^1.9.21` | TypeScript types for the above |
| `pdfjs-dist` | `^4.7.76` | Rendering PDF pages to images (georeferencing, PDF Print Mode, Map Cuts) |
| `tesseract.js` | `^5.1.1` | OCR — used by GeoreferencePanel's "detect" step (via `features/printkmz/ocr.ts`) |
| `jszip` | `^3.10.1` | Building `.kmz` files (zip of KML) for export |
| `polygon-clipping` | `^0.15.7` | Polygon union math for the Merge geometry op |
| `html2canvas` | `^1.4.1` | Rasterizing the map/report for PDF export |
| `jspdf` | `^4.2.1` | Building the exported PDF report |
| `xlsx` | `^0.18.5` | Bulk rate-card import (reachable from the billing picker) |
| `firebase` | `^12.15.0` | Real authentication (unrelated to map rendering) |
| `react` / `react-dom` | `^18.3.1` | — |
| `react-router-dom` | `^6.26.1` | Routing |
| `lucide-react` | `^0.427.0` | Icons |
| `i18next` / `react-i18next` | `^26.3.4` / `^17.0.8` | Add Work wizard is i18n-driven |

### Present in `package.json` but **not actually used by the Field Map**

- **`mapbox-gl` `^3.7.0`** — orphaned. Its only consumer (`src/components/MapView.tsx`) was deleted on 2026-07-01. Zero `mapbox-gl` imports remain anywhere in `src/`. Do not port this dependency when integrating — it's dead weight.
- **`@supabase/supabase-js` `^2.45.4`** — only used by the now-orphaned `features/printkmz/supabase.ts`, which itself has no live caller.

### Explicitly NOT dependencies (don't reach for these)

- No `react-leaflet` — the whole codebase talks to Leaflet's imperative API directly.
- No `@types/mapbox-gl` — CLAUDE.md itself warns against adding it (bundled types are relied on for the one place mapbox-gl types were needed, now moot).
- No state-management library (Redux/Zustand/etc.) — a single React Context (`DataContext`) is the entire store.

## 6. Environment Variables

Declared in `src/vite-env.d.ts`:

```ts
interface ImportMetaEnv {
  readonly VITE_MAPBOX_TOKEN?: string
  readonly VITE_SUPABASE_URL?: string
  readonly VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY?: string
  readonly VITE_DEFAULT_CENTER?: string
  readonly VITE_FIREBASE_API_KEY?: string
  readonly VITE_FIREBASE_AUTH_DOMAIN?: string
  readonly VITE_FIREBASE_PROJECT_ID?: string
  readonly VITE_FIREBASE_STORAGE_BUCKET?: string
  readonly VITE_FIREBASE_MESSAGING_SENDER_ID?: string
  readonly VITE_FIREBASE_APP_ID?: string
}
```

**For the Field Map feature specifically, none of these are required.** Here's the honest breakdown:

| Var | Required for Field Map? | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `VITE_FIREBASE_*` (6 vars) | Indirectly | Gates the whole app's login screen (real auth) — not specific to the map, but without it nobody gets past `Login`/`SetupWizard` to reach any page |
| `VITE_MAPBOX_TOKEN` | **No** | Dead — its only consumer was deleted. Field Map uses keyless OSM/Esri tiles. |
| `VITE_SUPABASE_URL` / `VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY` | **No** | Only referenced by orphaned `features/printkmz/supabase.ts`, which has no live caller |
| `VITE_DEFAULT_CENTER` | **No** | Only used by the orphaned printkmz store; `KmzMap.tsx` hardcodes its own default center |

**If you integrate just the Field Map into a new app, you need zero environment variables** to get the map, drawing, Work Objects, photos, billing, and production linkage working — it's all local-storage/IndexedDB-backed. You'd only need Firebase vars if you also port the login gate as-is.

---

## 7. Full Feature List

- Interactive Leaflet map (street/satellite base layers, no API key)
- ~90 drawing tools across 4 families: freeform primitives, generic feature-drop pins, structure markers, and discipline-specific engineering symbols
- Click-based (2-click line/polygon) and drag-based (pen/rect/circle/arrow/etc.) drawing gestures, each with full mouse **and** touch support
- Undo/redo (session-only, additions only)
- Guided "Add Work" wizard: Type → Draw → Details → Photos → Billing → Save, with a live completion checklist
- Per-Work-Type default color/unit/allowed-statuses/required-photo-phases/curated-tool-list/inspection-template
- Mandatory-field validation gate before a Work Object counts as "complete" (comments, status, crew, quantity, GPS, required photos, ≥1 billable line)
- GPS capture via browser Geolocation API, with an explicit "confirm unavailable" override
- Photo/video/attachment upload — device camera (mobile) or file picker (desktop) — stored in IndexedDB, linked by markup id and required-photo "phase"
- Billing lines per markup: unit code / description / unit type / quantity / rate / total / billable flag / invoice status, sourced from the customer Rate Card catalog
- "Submit to Production" — turns billable, not-yet-billed billing lines into `ProductionEntry` + `ProductionLineItem` + `PnLEntry` records, grouped by crew
- **Two-way live sync after submission**: editing a billing line's quantity/rate later updates the already-created Production/P&L records in place (no duplicate entries, no stale numbers)
- QA/QC inspection checklists per markup, seeded from the work type's template, with pass/fail/na per item and a computed overall result
- Full audit history per markup (every field change, photo/billing/inspection add/remove, lock/unlock, delete)
- Soft-delete with full cascade to Production/P&L (and hard-block if any billing line has already been invoiced)
- Auto-generated, always-current callout boxes for Work Objects (crew/quantity/date/billing-code/status — deliberately no dollar figures or GPS) plus manually-typed free callouts
- Draggable, resizable callout boxes and a draggable floating properties panel (position persisted per-markup)
- Split/Merge geometry operations on lines and polygons
- Snap-to-vertex while drawing
- Layer visibility manager (Work Object layers, KMZ-imported layers, georeferenced overlays)
- PDF plan georeferencing: upload a PDF, pick 3+ control points, solve an affine transform, overlay the page image on the live map at the correct scale/position/rotation
- OCR-assisted candidate Work Object detection on a georeferenced PDF page, with draggable confirm/dismiss pins
- KMZ/KML export (both KMZ-imported features and hand-drawn markups)
- Aerial Lash Fiber run tracking — per-pole tick marks/notes/photos along a strand run
- Locking a markup (freezes edits/deletes without touching data)
- Mobile-specific bottom nav + floating "+Add Work" FAB, fully responsive down to phone width
- Map/report PDF export (snapshot + Work Object summary table)

---

## 8. Setup and Installation

Since there is no backend, setup is just "run the frontend":

```bash
npm install
npm run dev        # Vite dev server, http://localhost:5173
npm run build       # tsc -b && vite build → dist/
npm run preview     # serve the production build
npm run lint        # eslint, max-warnings 0
npm run typecheck   # tsc --noEmit
```

**To integrate just the Field Map into another React app**, the minimum viable slice is:

1. Copy `src/types.ts`'s Field-Map-relevant interfaces (§4) into your domain model.
2. Stand up a `DataContext`-equivalent store exposing the CRUD methods listed in §3/§22 (`addMarkup`, `updateMarkup`, `deleteMarkup`, `softDeleteMarkup`, `addMarkupPhoto`/`deleteMarkupPhoto`, `addMarkupBilling`/`updateMarkupBilling`/`deleteMarkupBilling`, `addMarkupVideo`/`deleteMarkupVideo`, `addMarkupInspection`/`updateMarkupInspection`/`deleteMarkupInspection`, `addMarkupAttachment`/`deleteMarkupAttachment`, `addFieldMapOverlay`/`updateFieldMapOverlay`/`deleteFieldMapOverlay`, `addProduction`).
3. Port `src/lib/fileStore.ts` verbatim (zero external deps — just raw IndexedDB).
4. Port `KmzMap.tsx`, `MarkupPanel.tsx`, `AddWorkModal.tsx`, `WorkObjectPropertiesPanel.tsx`, `MarkupQuickActions.tsx`, `AddWorkDiscardConfirm.tsx`, `MarkupDeleteConfirm.tsx`, `GeoreferencePanel.tsx`, `LayerManagerPanel.tsx`, `FieldMapToolbar.tsx`, `SymbolIcon.tsx`, `PhotoImg.tsx` and their `src/lib/` dependencies (§1's table).
5. Install `leaflet`, `pdfjs-dist`, `tesseract.js`, `jszip`, `polygon-clipping` (skip `mapbox-gl`).
6. No environment variables needed for this slice (§6).

See §26 for a fuller step-by-step integration guide.

---

## 9. How PDF Maps Work

There are **two distinct PDF-handling paths** in this codebase — keep them separate when rebuilding.

### 9a. On the live Field Map itself: `GeoreferencePanel.tsx`

This is a non-blocking side panel opened from within `KmzMap.tsx` — the Leaflet map stays interactive underneath while calibrating. Workflow:

1. **Upload**: pick a PDF (or it auto-preloads from a "Georeference to Map" action started in PDF Print Mode). The first page is rasterized via `renderPdf()` (reused from `features/printkmz/pdf.ts`).
2. **Calibrate**: click a point on the rendered `<img>`, then click the matching real-world spot on the Leaflet map — repeat for **3 or more control points**.
3. **Save**: the image is stored in IndexedDB, and a `GeoreferencedOverlay` record is created holding the raw `controlPoints` array (not a pre-baked transform, so recalibration stays possible).

The transform math (`src/lib/georeference.ts`) is a small, dependency-free affine solver:

```ts
export interface GeoTransform { a: number; b: number; c: number; d: number; e: number; f: number }
// lng = a*x + b*y + c ; lat = d*x + e*y + f

export function computeTransform(points: ControlPoint[]): GeoTransform {
  if (points.length < 2) throw new Error('At least 2 control points are required')
  if (points.length === 2) return similarityFromTwoPoints(points[0], points[1])  // scale+rotation only
  return affineLeastSquares(points)   // 3+ points: full affine (handles skew), via 3x3 normal equations
}
```

`computeScreenMatrix()` composes this geo transform with Leaflet's own `latLngToContainerPoint` projection into CSS `matrix()` coefficients, so the rendered PDF page image can be positioned/warped directly via a CSS transform on every map pan/zoom — cheap, no re-render of the image itself needed, because (per the code's own comment) "Web Mercator has no rotation at a fixed zoom, so two composed affine maps are still affine."

A bonus: any Work Objects drawn earlier in PDF Print Mode on the *same* source PDF page (tagged `coordSpace: 'pdfPage'`) get automatically re-projected to real `lat/lng` the moment the page is georeferenced (`convertPdfMarkupsToLatLng()`), including correctly re-measuring circle radii from Euclidean PDF-point units to true geodesic meters.

An optional **OCR "detect" step** follows: `tesseract.js` runs over the rasterized page, candidate Work Objects are seeded at their OCR'd pixel position (projected through the same transform to lat/lng) as **draggable** pins, which the user drags to the exact spot and then Confirms (creates a real `FieldMarkup`) or Dismisses.

### 9b. PDF Print Mode (`src/pages/PdfPrintMode.tsx`)

A sibling page (`/kmz/:projectId/print/:fileId`) for redlining directly on a static PDF page image instead of the live map — useful before a plan is georeferenced, or for offline-style paper-replacement workflows. It uses the same `FieldMarkup` records (tagged `coordSpace: 'pdfPage'`) and the same `markupToPdfSvg.tsx` renderer (mirrors `markupLayer.ts`'s rules, but emits SVG instead of Leaflet layers) and `PdfCalloutOverlay.tsx` (a stateless React port of KmzMap's callout DOM overlay).

### 9c. What's dead (don't rebuild this)

`src/features/printkmz/` used to have a full "upload PDF → OCR → drag candidates onto a Mapbox GL map → export KMZ" pipeline via `PrintReader.tsx`/`PrintReview.tsx`/`MapView.tsx`/`ObjectEditorDrawer.tsx`. **All four were deleted 2026-07-01.** Of the remaining `features/printkmz/` files, only `pdf.ts` (page rasterization) and `ocr.ts`'s `runOcrWithBoxes` are still live — reused by `GeoreferencePanel` and `MapReading.tsx`. `detect.ts`, `kmz.ts`, `kmzImport.ts`, `store.ts`, and `supabase.ts` in that folder have **zero remaining callers** — don't port them.

---

## 10. How KMZ/KML Maps Work

**Import and export are two separate code paths, on two separate pages.**

### Import — `src/pages/KmzProduction.tsx` + `src/lib/kmzParser.ts`

`KmzMap.tsx` has **no upload/import UI of its own**. A user uploads a `.kmz`/`.kml` file on the `KmzProduction.tsx` landing page; `parseKmzOrKml(file)` (a 500+ line parser handling `Style`/`StyleMap`/`ExtendedData`/name-heuristics/embedded icons) converts it into `MapFeature[]`, which get stored via `addKmzUpload(...)`. `KmzMap.tsx` then only **reads** `data.mapFeatures`/`data.kmzUploads` that were already imported upstream — imported KMZ shapes render as read-mostly `MapFeature` records (opened in `FeaturePanel.tsx`), a completely different entity from a hand-drawn `FieldMarkup`.

### Export — `src/lib/kmzExport.ts`, buttons live in `KmzMap.tsx`

Two independent export functions, both building a `doc.kml` and zipping it with JSZip:

- `exportFeaturesToKmz(features, projectName)` — exports the currently-visible **imported** `MapFeature[]`, grouped into a `<Folder>` per layer name, with one `<Style>` per status.
- `exportFieldMarkupsToKmz(markups, projectName)` — exports **hand-drawn** `FieldMarkup[]`, one `<Style>` per unique color, with `<ExtendedData>` carrying `colorCode`/`workType`/`assetType`/`lengthFt`.

Both funnel through `triggerDownload()` for the browser download.

---

## 11. How Redlines and Markups Work

Every redline/markup is a `FieldMarkup` record (§4), and the single conversion function from that record to something visible on the map is `markupToLayer(m, map)` in `src/lib/markupLayer.ts`.

### Style resolution

```ts
const color = m.color || '#ef4444'
const opts: L.PathOptions = { color, weight: m.weight || 3, opacity: m.opacity ?? 0.95, pane: 'markups', dashArray: dashArrayFor(m) }
```

Dash-array priority is explicit and three-tiered: `FieldMarkup.lineStyle` (user override) → the tool's own entry in the engineering-symbol catalog → a small legacy per-tool fallback map.

### Hit-area widening

Leaflet's native click hit-area on a thin polyline is hard to tap, especially on touch. `withWideHitArea()` pairs the real styled line with an invisible ≥20px-wide companion polyline (`opacity: 0.01`, forced `pointer-events: stroke`) inside an `L.featureGroup`.

### Per-tool rendering, by category

- **Lines** (`pen|line|dashed_line|dotted_line|multi_line|measure|highlight`) → plain `L.polyline` + wide hit area.
- **Arrows** (`direction_arrow|arrow|double_arrow`) → an `L.layerGroup` of the polyline plus a CSS-triangle `divIcon` rotated to the line's bearing.
- **Rect** → `L.rectangle`. **Circle** → `L.circle` (radius in meters). **Ellipse** → Leaflet has no native ellipse, so it's an `L.svgOverlay` of a raw `<svg><ellipse>` bound to the shape's bounds (scales/positions automatically like `L.rectangle` does).
- **Polygon/cloud** → `L.polygon` (a "cloud" is a deliberate simplification: thick dashed round-joined stroke, not true bezier scallops).
- **Text** → `L.marker` + `divIcon` honoring font family/size/bold/italic/underline/strikethrough.
- **Callout** → returns `null` from this function; it's rendered as a separate screen-fixed DOM overlay directly in `KmzMap.tsx` (§18), not a Leaflet layer.
- **Everything else** (feature drops, engineering symbols, `struct_*` markers) → a `divIcon` built from the shared symbol-shape catalog.

### Engineering symbol catalog — `src/lib/engineeringSymbols.ts`

One shared catalog (`ENGINEERING_SYMBOLS`) drives three consumers: toolbar icons (`SymbolIcon.tsx`), Leaflet rendering (`markupLayer.ts`), and PDF SVG rendering (`markupToPdfSvg.tsx`) — "adding a new symbol only ever means adding a catalog entry here," per its own header comment. Each entry has a `tool`, `label`, `abbr` (short text drawn inside the shape), `color`, `geometryKind` (`'point'|'line'`), and either a `shape` (point tools: `hexagon|circleDot|diamond|flag|oval|coil|cross|square|pinBadge`) or a `lineStyle` (line tools: `solid|dashed|dotted|tickMarked|arrowTerminated`), plus a `variant` (`'existing'|'new'`) that toggles hollow-outline vs. solid-fill — the standard drafting convention. Shapes are rendered as inline SVG strings (not canvas), interpolated into a Leaflet `divIcon`'s `html`.

Engineering-symbol *line* tools with `lineStyle: 'tickMarked'` additionally draw perpendicular tick segments computed by `computeTickMarks()` — a unit-agnostic function (works in both lat/lng-degree space and PDF page-point space) shared by both the Leaflet and PDF SVG renderers.

---

## 12. How Work Types Are Selected

`WorkObjectTypeDef` (`src/lib/workObjectTypes.ts`):

```ts
export interface WorkObjectTypeDef {
  id: WorkObjectTypeId
  label: string
  shortCode: string                 // e.g. "TRN" → generates Work IDs like "WO-TRN-014"
  icon: LucideIcon
  defaultColor: string
  defaultGeometry: 'point' | 'line' | 'polygon'
  defaultMarkupTool: MarkupTool
  defaultUnit: 'Feet' | 'Each' | 'SqFt'
  billingKeywords: string[]
  requiredPhotoPhases: PhotoProofType[]
  requiresNotes: boolean
  allowedStatuses: MarkupStatus[]
  inspectionTemplate: string[]      // seeds a new Inspection checklist
}
```

There are **22 entries** (`trenching`, `handhole_vault`, `aerial_strand`, `directional_drill`, `distribution_fiber`, `feeder_fiber`, `drop`, `plowing`, `sub_ducting`, `pole`, `anchor_down_guy`, `splicing`, `restoration`, `qa_qc`, `utility_conflict`, `damage_report`, `flower_pot`, `tie_in`, `riser_guard`, `road_crossing`, `sidewalk_crossing`, `driveway_crossing`, `other`). Three of these (`qa_qc`, `utility_conflict`, `damage_report`) use a restricted `NON_BILLABLE_STATUSES` set (no `approved`/`billed`) since they're issue-report types, not billable construction.

Picking a type applies its defaults immediately, from either `AddWorkModal.tsx` (Step 1) or `WorkObjectPropertiesPanel.tsx` (editing later) — identical logic in both places:

```ts
const patch: Partial<FieldMarkup> = { workObjectType: newType.id, color: newType.defaultColor, unit: newType.defaultUnit }
if (!newType.allowedStatuses.includes(markup.status)) patch.status = newType.allowedStatuses[0]
```

`requiredPhotoPhases` feeds the Photos-step validation, and `inspectionTemplate` seeds a new Inspection tab's checklist. `relevantToolsForWorkType(typeId)` (also in `workObjectTypes.ts`) curates which drawing tools the toolbar shows once a type is picked, via an explicit per-type override list or a fallback by `defaultGeometry`.

---

## 13. How Drawing Tools Work

### Two mutually-exclusive interaction models

**Click-accumulation** — for `line | polygon | multi_line | measure | cloud` and any "line-accumulation" engineering symbol tool. Each click pushes a point; a live rubber-band preview redraws on mouse/touch move; finishing is via double-click, `Enter`, or (for `line`, always exactly 2 points) auto-finish on the 2nd click. `Escape` cancels. A "drag to place both points in one gesture" ergonomic shortcut is also supported (6px movement threshold to distinguish from a tap).

**Drag-to-draw** — for `pen | highlight | dashed_line | dotted_line | arrow | double_arrow | rect | ellipse | circle`. Implemented via a **transparent overlay `<div>` sitting on top of the map** only while a drag-draw tool is active — critically, this means Leaflet's own mousedown/move/up handlers never fire (the overlay intercepts them first), so Leaflet's native pan/drag behavior stays completely undisturbed in Select mode. `drawStart`/`drawMove`/`drawEnd` manipulate a live Leaflet preview layer directly from raw mouse/touch coordinates.

Both models have **fully parallel touch handling** (not a shared abstraction — separate `onTouchStart/Move/End` handlers mirroring the mouse ones 1:1), plus a native-tap listener directly on the Leaflet container for feature-drop/single-click tools, since "Leaflet's built-in tap→click conversion can miss taps or fire too late" on phones.

### Commit — every drawing path ends at one function

```ts
const commitMarkup = useCallback((partial: Omit<FieldMarkup, 'id'|'createdAt'|'projectId'|'status'|'layer'|'crewId'|'createdBy'|'updatedAt'|'lockedAt'>) => {
  const id = addMarkup({ ...partial, projectId, status: 'pending', layer: rlLayer, crewId: null, createdBy: null, updatedAt: null, lockedAt: null })
  undoStackRef.current.push(id)
  redoStackRef.current = []
  setActiveTool('select')   // always snap back to Select
  setTimeout(() => {
    if (addWorkModeRef.current) {
      // user picked a Work Object type first — open the Add Work wizard on this new shape
      setAddWorkMarkupId(id); setAddWorkModalOpen(true)
    } else {
      // freeform drawing — select the shape and open the right-side panel
      setSelectedMarkup(...); setPanelCollapsed(false)
    }
  }, 50)
}, [...])
```

Line length (`lengthFt`) is computed by a local haversine helper for every line/pen/measure/multi_line commit.

### Undo/redo

Session-only (plain refs, not persisted): `undoStackRef` holds ids of markups added this session; `redoStackRef` holds full snapshots popped off it. Undo is a **hard delete** (`deleteMarkup`, never `softDeleteMarkup`) since nothing drawn-and-undone this session could have been billed yet. Redo simply re-`addMarkup`s the popped snapshot (which mints a fresh id/createdAt).

Editing an already-committed shape's geometry (moving vertices, resizing, moving the whole shape) is handled separately by `buildEditHandles`/`buildSplitVertexMarkers` in `markupLayer.ts` — draggable handle markers that call `updateMarkup({ geometry: ... })` on release.

---

## 14. How GPS Is Captured and Saved

GPS is **not** part of the drawing gesture — it's a separate, explicit action taken later, in the Add Work wizard's Details step:

```ts
function captureGps() {
  if (!markup || !navigator.geolocation) { setGpsStatus('error'); return }
  setGpsStatus('capturing')
  navigator.geolocation.getCurrentPosition(
    (pos) => {
      patchMarkup({ capturedLat: pos.coords.latitude, capturedLng: pos.coords.longitude, gpsUnavailableConfirmed: false })
      setGpsStatus('idle')
    },
    () => setGpsStatus('error'),
    { enableHighAccuracy: true, timeout: 10000 },
  )
}
```

- Uses the standard browser `Geolocation` API — requires the browser's own location-permission prompt.
- On success: writes `FieldMarkup.capturedLat`/`capturedLng` (explicitly documented as distinct from the drawn geometry's own coordinates — this is "where the device physically was" vs. "where the shape was drawn on the map").
- On failure (denied, unsupported, or timeout): only a local `gpsStatus` flag flips to `'error'` — nothing is written to the record. The UI then offers a "confirm GPS unavailable" button that sets `gpsUnavailableConfirmed: true`, satisfying validation without a real fix; this flag auto-clears if a real capture later succeeds.
- **Validation**: a Work Object cannot be marked wizard-complete unless `capturedLat`/`capturedLng` are both set, **or** `gpsUnavailableConfirmed` is true.

---

## 15. How Mandatory Work Forms Work

`AddWorkModal.tsx` gates completion behind three independent validators, all of which must pass:

```ts
function detailsErrors() {
  const errs = {}
  if (!markup.workObjectType) errs.workObjectType = '...'
  if (!markup.notes?.trim())  errs.comments = '...'          // ALWAYS required, regardless of requiresNotes
  if (!markup.status)         errs.status = '...'
  if (!markup.crewId)         errs.crew = '...'
  if (markup.quantity == null) errs.quantity = '...'
  const hasGps = markup.capturedLat != null && markup.capturedLng != null
  if (!hasGps && !markup.gpsUnavailableConfirmed) errs.gps = '...'
  return errs
}
function photosErrors() {
  return typeDef.requiredPhotoPhases.filter((phase) => !photos.some((p) => p.phase === phase))
}
function billingErrors() {
  if (billingLines.length === 0) return '...'
  if (!billingLines.some((b) => b.quantity > 0)) return '...'
  return null
}
const isWizardComplete = Object.keys(detailsErrors()).length === 0 && photosErrors().length === 0 && !billingErrors()
```

**Known inconsistency worth knowing about**: `WorkObjectTypeDef.requiresNotes` is *not* actually wired into `detailsErrors()` — comments are unconditionally required for every work type already. The per-type flag today only changes the placeholder text in the Comments textarea. If you rebuild this, decide deliberately whether you want per-type-optional comments (and wire the flag in) or keep them always-required (and consider removing the now-cosmetic-only flag).

A closely related but **separate, non-blocking** form is the **Inspection** checklist inside `MarkupPanel.tsx`'s `inspection` tab — seeded from `typeDef.inspectionTemplate` (or a `['General condition']` fallback), each item gets a pass/fail/na toggle, and the overall result is computed as `fail` if any item failed, `pass` if every item has a non-`na` result, else `pending`. Inspections are unlimited-count and never gate wizard completion.

---

## 16. How Photos Are Uploaded and Linked

### Capture

Two entry points, both converging on the same handler:
- A persistent hidden `<input type="file" accept="image/*,video/*" multiple>` — plain file/gallery picker (works on desktop or mobile).
- A **camera-specific** button (`MarkupPanel.tsx`'s Photos tab) that dynamically constructs a throwaway `<input type="file" accept="image/*" capture="environment">` and clicks it programmatically — the `capture="environment"` attribute is what forces the rear camera to open directly on mobile browsers (a no-op, safely ignored, on desktop).

### Storage

Every picked file is read via `FileReader.readAsDataURL`, then:
```ts
reader.onload = async (ev) => {
  const dataUrl = ev.target.result
  if (file.type.startsWith('video/')) {
    const id = addMarkupVideo({ markupId, caption: null, takenAt: new Date().toISOString() })
    await saveBlob(`mkp-${id}`, dataUrl)
  } else {
    const id = addMarkupPhoto({ markupId, caption: null, takenAt: new Date().toISOString(), uploadedBy, lat: null, lng: null, phase }, activeEmployeeId)
    await saveBlob(`mkp-${id}`, dataUrl)
  }
}
```
The data URL itself goes into IndexedDB (`fileStore.ts`) under key `mkp-<recordId>` — never into the localStorage-backed JSON blob. Attachments (generic files, e.g. permits) use the identical `mkp-<id>` convention.

### Linking

`MarkupPhoto.markupId` is the FK. `MarkupPhoto.phase` (a `PhotoProofType`, e.g. `'before'`/`'depth_proof'`/`'after'`/`'handhole_proof'`/etc.) is what `AddWorkModal.photosErrors()` checks off against the work type's `requiredPhotoPhases`.

### Display / delete

`PhotoThumb` components call `loadBlob('mkp-' + id)` in a `useEffect` and render an `<img>` once resolved (pulsing placeholder while loading); `MarkupPanel.tsx`'s version supports a full-screen lightbox on click. `deleteMarkupPhoto(id, actor)` removes the `MarkupPhoto` record, marks the parent markup `syncStatus: 'pending'`, writes a `photo_removed` audit entry, and calls `deleteBlob('mkp-' + id)` to actually free the IndexedDB entry.

---

## 17. How Units, Quantity, Crew, Status, and Comments Are Saved

| Concept | Field | Type | Edited in |
|---|---|---|---|
| Unit (human label) | `FieldMarkup.unit` | `string?` (e.g. `"Feet"`) | Set automatically from the chosen Work Type's `defaultUnit` |
| Quantity | `FieldMarkup.quantity` | `number \| null` | `AddWorkModal.tsx` (Details step) and `WorkObjectPropertiesPanel.tsx` |
| Crew | `FieldMarkup.crewId` | `string \| null` (FK → Crew) | Same two places |
| Status | `FieldMarkup.status` | `MarkupStatus` (non-optional) | Same two places |
| Comments/notes | `FieldMarkup.notes` | `string \| null` | `AddWorkModal.tsx` and `MarkupPanel.tsx`'s Details tab **only** — not editable in `WorkObjectPropertiesPanel` |
| Billing unit/quantity/rate | `MarkupBilling.unitType/quantity/rate/total` | independent record, one markup can have several | `AddWorkModal.tsx`'s Billing step and `MarkupPanel.tsx`'s Billing tab |

Two different save mechanisms exist side by side, and it matters which one you're integrating:

- **`WorkObjectPropertiesPanel.tsx`** writes **immediately on every change** — no dirty-tracking, no Save button:
  ```ts
  onChange={(e) => patch({ quantity: e.target.value === '' ? null : Number(e.target.value) })}
  // where patch(p) = updateMarkup(markup.id, p, activeEmployeeId)
  ```
- **`MarkupPanel.tsx`'s Details tab** stages `featureName`/`notes` in local component state and requires an explicit **Save** click (disabled unless `dirty && !isLocked`):
  ```ts
  function saveDetails() {
    updateMarkup(markup.id, { featureName: featureName || null, notes: notes || null }, activeEmployeeId)
    setDirty(false)
  }
  ```
  Everything else in that same panel (color/weight/opacity/lineStyle/font fields) writes immediately, bypassing the dirty/Save gate entirely — only `featureName`/`notes` are staged.

**Important nuance for a rebuild**: `FieldMarkup.quantity` only **seeds** a new `MarkupBilling.quantity` once, at the moment a billing line is first added (`quantity = markup.quantity ?? 1`). After that, editing the markup's `quantity` field will — in this codebase's current implementation — cascade forward into any billing line still equal to the old value (and further into Production/P&L if already submitted), but a billing line that was ever manually edited to a different number is treated as independent from then on and won't follow further markup-quantity changes. See §22 for the exact cascade logic.

---

## 18. How Callout Boxes Work

There are **two independent callout mechanisms**, both rendered from `KmzMap.tsx` directly as DOM overlays (not Leaflet layers, since Leaflet has no native "always-readable-size, offset-from-anchor" primitive).

### Manual, free-typed callouts (`tool: 'callout'`)

Created via the same single-click path as the `text` tool. Its content lives directly on `FieldMarkup.label` — a plain, user-typed string. Shows an inline `✕` that triggers the normal soft-delete flow.

### Auto-generated Work Object callouts

Every markup with a `workObjectType` automatically gets a companion callout, anchored at the shape's geometric center/midpoint/vertex-average (`geometryAnchor()`). Its text is **computed fresh on every render, never stored** — this is what makes it "always current" for free, since editing the markup's fields (crew, quantity, status, billing) is reflected instantly without any separate sync step:

```ts
// src/lib/workObjectCallout.ts — deliberately field-crew-facing only:
// no Work ID, GPS, Notes, or any dollar figures.
return [
  typeDef?.label ?? markup.tool,
  `Crew: ${crewName ?? 'Unassigned'}`,
  `Quantity: ${quantityLine}`,
  `Date: ${new Date(markup.createdAt).toLocaleDateString()}`,
  `Billing Code: ${billingCodeLines.join(', ') || '—'}`,
  `Status: ${MARKUP_STATUS_META[markup.status]?.label ?? markup.status}`,
]
```
(Billing lines show `${rateCode} x ${quantity}` only — never a dollar amount — truncated to 2 lines with a "+N more" suffix.)

### Position/resize mechanics

A single `renderCallout()` closure handles both kinds. Deliberately does **not** scale with zoom (unlike every other markup layer) — a callout's whole job is to stay legibly sized, so only its **anchor point** is re-derived per frame (via `latLngToContainerPoint`) while the box itself keeps a constant on-screen size and constant pixel offset.

- **Drag-to-reposition**: on mousedown, temporarily disables `map.dragging`, tracks the delta live, and distinguishes a drag from a click via a movement threshold (15px in KmzMap, 6px in the PDF-mode equivalent) — a non-drag click selects the linked markup instead of moving the callout.
- **Resize**: a custom `position:fixed` handle (not native CSS `resize`) tracked the same way.
- **Position/size persistence is session-only** — held in a plain `Map` inside a `useRef` (`calloutStateRef`), **not** written to `DataContext`/localStorage. Reloading the page resets every callout to its default offset.
- A shared `<svg class="callout-arrows">` draws a dashed leader line with an arrowhead from the box to its anchor.

### A *different*, actually-persisted position store

Don't confuse the callout's ephemeral position with `src/lib/workObjectPanelPosition.ts` — this is the **floating properties panel's** (`WorkObjectPropertiesPanel.tsx`) drag position, which **is** saved, in its own tiny dedicated `localStorage` key (`fiberlytic:workObjectPanelPos:v1`), keyed by markup id, deliberately separate from `AppData` since "only one panel is ever mounted at a time, so there's no concurrent-consumer scenario to justify" folding it into the main store.

### PDF Print Mode's equivalent

`src/components/PdfCalloutOverlay.tsx` is a plain, stateless React component (no imperative map to hang listeners off) that reproduces the same visual behavior — parent-owned anchor state, identical drag-threshold logic, but position math is delegated to the parent via an `onMove(id, offsetX, offsetY)` callback rather than owning a ref map itself.

---

## 19. How Delete/Edit Workflows Work

### Two different delete functions, deliberately

**`softDeleteMarkup(id, actor)`** — the **only** user-facing "Delete this Work Object" path (toolbar, layer manager, `MarkupPanel`'s Delete button, quick-actions, the Delete key, a manual callout's inline ✕):
```ts
softDeleteMarkup(id, actor = null) {
  // 1. Flags the markup deletedAt/deletedBy — the record, its photos, billing, and history all survive for audit.
  // 2. Finds every ProductionEntry with sourceMarkupId === id, and HARD-removes those entries,
  //    their PnLEntry, their ProductionLineItems, and any production-linked photo blobs.
  // 3. Recomputes each affected project's footageComplete.
  // 4. Appends a 'deleted' audit entry.
}
```
Before staging a delete, the shared flow (`src/lib/markupDelete.ts`) checks `isMarkupInvoiced(billingLines)` — if any billing line already has an `invoiceId`, it **blocks entirely** with an alert ("Void or credit that invoice before deleting it"), since an issued invoice is a frozen snapshot that can't be silently un-invoiced.

**`deleteMarkup(id)`** — a hard delete used **only internally**, never as the user-facing action: removes the markup **and its own audit history**, plus photos/billing/videos/inspections/attachments. Used by: `undoLast()` (nothing billed yet), Split/Merge (replacing originals with their derived pieces), a manual `callout`'s own delete (no billing/production ever exists for a callout), and `AddWorkModal`'s discard-draft action (nothing submitted yet).

### The confirmation modal

`MarkupDeleteConfirm.tsx` explicitly lists the blast radius to the user: *"This will remove it from: Map, Production, P&L, Dashboard, Billing, Reports"* — *"The item stays in audit history but no longer appears anywhere above."*

### Edit workflows

- Field-level edits go through `updateMarkup(id, patch, actor)`, which diffs the patch and writes one `field_changed` audit entry per changed field (via `diffMarkupUpdate`), except for a few dedicated action types (`locked`/`unlocked` get their own entries instead).
- Geometry edits (move/resize/reshape an already-drawn shape) go through draggable handle markers built by `buildEditHandles`/`buildSplitVertexMarkers` in `markupLayer.ts`, which call `updateMarkup({ geometry: ... })` on release.
- **Locking** (`toggleLock()` in `MarkupPanel.tsx`) sets/clears `lockedAt` — while locked, edit/upload/save/delete buttons across the panel are disabled. This is a workflow lock, not a security permission (see §24 — there is no role-based gating in this feature at all).

---

## 20. How Map Cuts and Grid Cuts Work

**Map Cuts is a separate tool** (`src/pages/MapCuts.tsx`) — *"Slice one oversized plan sheet into a readable, multi-page field print package."* It shares no code and no data with the Field Map's `FieldMarkup`/`GeoreferencedOverlay` records; the only connection is a manual, user-initiated "Open in Print Mode" button after generating cut sheets.

### Data model

```ts
export type MapCutStyle = 'grid' | 'manual' | 'assistedRoute'  // assistedRoute is reserved, not implemented
export type MapCutPageSize = '11x17' | '8.5x11' | 'legal' | 'ansiC' | 'ansiD' | 'custom'

export interface MapCutBox {
  id: string
  x: number; y: number; width: number; height: number  // 0–1 fractions of the source page, pre-rotation
  rotation: number
  order: number          // 1-based output order ("Sheet N")
}
export interface GridCellSelection {
  rows: number; cols: number
  selectedOrder: Record<string, number>  // cellId "row-col" → click order
  merges: string[][]                     // groups of cellIds merged into one output box
}
export interface MapCutPackage {
  id: string; name: string; projectId: string
  sourceProjectFileId: string | null; sourceFileName: string; sourcePageIndex: number
  cutStyle: MapCutStyle; pageSize: MapCutPageSize
  customWidthIn?: number; customHeightIn?: number
  gridRows?: number; gridCols?: number
  overlapPct: number                     // 0–30%, applied at generate time, never baked into stored geometry
  boxes: MapCutBox[]
  gridDirty: boolean; gridSelection?: GridCellSelection
  scaleFeetPerInch?: number
  projectNameOverride?: string; notes?: string; productionNotes?: string; detectedTitle?: string
  outputProjectFileId?: string | null; outputFileName: string
  outputDpi?: 300 | 600 | 1200; losslessOutput?: boolean
  createdAt: string; updatedAt: string
}
```

### Grid-cut algorithm (`src/lib/mapCuts/geometry.ts`)

1. `computeGridCells(rows, cols)` produces unexpanded fractional rects, keyed `"${row}-${col}"`.
2. The user clicks cells to select them (recorded in `selectedOrder`, a monotonic counter so deselecting never renumbers everything), and can merge adjacent cells (`areCellsConnected()` validates edge-connectivity via flood-fill before allowing a merge).
3. `gridSelectionToBoxes(selection)` finalizes the selection into `MapCutBox[]`: one box per merge group or lone cell, rect = the bounding union of its member cells, `order` assigned 1..N by ascending minimum click-order.
4. `expandRect(rect, overlapPct)` grows each box around its own center by the overlap percentage (0–30%), clamped to the page — applied at render/generate time, not stored, so overlap can be re-tuned after the fact.

Manual Cut mode is entirely separate draw/move/resize/rotate code with its own id scheme, explicitly noted as sharing nothing with the grid path.

### Rendering and output

`src/lib/mapCuts/render.ts`'s `renderViewportRegion()` renders any rectangular region of the source PDF page at any scale, directly from the vector source (never from a shared fixed-resolution raster) — reused for the interactive viewport, the locator thumbnail, and the final full-DPI export.

`src/lib/mapCuts/pdfBuilder.ts`'s `buildMapCutPdf()` generates the final output: for each box (in `order`), it picks the output page's physical dimensions (auto-flipping orientation to match the box's own aspect ratio), renders that box's rotated region directly from the vector PDF at `outputDpi/72` scale (clamped to a 16384px canvas-size limit), rotate-crops it out via canvas transforms, and places it in the page area below a reserved title-block band.

`src/lib/mapCuts/titleBlock.ts` draws that band: project name / source file+page / **"Sheet N of Total"**, notes/production-notes text, a north arrow, an optional scale bar (built from `scaleFeetPerInch` via a "pick a nice round foot value" step table), and a locator thumbnail of the full source page with a red rectangle marking this cut's location.

The finished PDF is saved back as a **new** `ProjectFile` via `addProjectFile` — Map Cuts never touches `fieldMarkups` or `fieldMapOverlays` directly.

---

## 21. How Scale and Measurement Tools Work

**There is no scale-bar/ruler HUD on the live Leaflet Field Map.** This is worth stating plainly since it's easy to assume one exists.

The only measurement-flavored tool is `MarkupTool = 'measure'` — but it is **not** a distinct interaction or overlay; it's rendered and drawn identically to a plain line/polyline (grouped with `pen|line|dashed_line|dotted_line|multi_line|highlight` in every switch statement that matters). Its only distinguishing behavior is that its computed `lengthFt` (via the same haversine helper every line tool uses) is surfaced as a labeled figure in `MarkupPanel.tsx`. There is no live distance tooltip that follows the cursor while measuring, no unit toggle, and no "measure and discard" ephemeral mode — a `measure`-tool shape is a first-class, persisted `FieldMarkup` exactly like any other drawn shape.

There is also no `L.control.scale` (Leaflet's built-in scale-bar control) anywhere in this codebase.

The actual "scale" concept in this feature area is the **georeferencing control-point system** (§9) — it solves a coordinate transform (mapping PDF pixels to real lat/lng, which implicitly encodes scale/rotation/position all at once) rather than displaying a measurement. Two unrelated "scale" fields exist elsewhere for different features entirely: `ProjectFile.pdfScaleFeetPerInch` and `MapCutPackage.scaleFeetPerInch` (§20) — both belong to Map Cuts/PDF Print Mode, not the live Field Map.

---

## 22. How Production Data Connects to P&L/Revenue

This is the most consequential piece of business logic in the whole feature — get this right in a rebuild, since it's where hand-drawn field data becomes real dollars.

### The submission function

```ts
// src/lib/productionFromMarkup.ts — submitMarkupToProduction()
export function submitMarkupToProduction(args): { submitted: true } | null {
  const { markup, billingEntries, activeEmployeeId, data, addProduction, updateMarkupBilling, updateMarkup } = args

  // De-dup gate: only lines never yet submitted can be submitted again.
  const billable = billingEntries.filter((b) => b.billable && b.total > 0 && b.invoiceStatus === 'not_billed')
  if (billable.length === 0) return null

  // Crew fallback: markup.crewId → active employee's default crew → project's first crew
  const resolvedCrewId = markup.crewId || employeeDefaultCrewId || projectFirstCrewId || ''

  // Group billing lines by crew — each crew gets its OWN ProductionEntry
  const byCrewId = groupBy(billable, (b) => b.crewId || resolvedCrewId)

  for (const [crewId, lines] of byCrewId) {
    const lineItems = lines.map((b) => ({
      unitCode: b.rateCode || 'MISC', description: b.description, uom: b.unitType,
      quantity: b.quantity, rateSnapshot: b.rate, extendedTotal: b.total,
      sourceMarkupBillingId: b.id,   // ← the FK that makes later edits reconcile correctly
    }))
    // Footage prefers the actual billed LF quantity over the drawn line's raw length,
    // and only the FIRST crew processed can fall back to markup.lengthFt (never double-counted).
    const footage = lfQuantitySum(lines) || (isFirstCrew ? markup.lengthFt ?? 0 : 0)
    addProduction({ date: today, projectId: markup.projectId, crewId, footage, hours: 0, notes: baseNotes, sourceMarkupId: markup.id }, lineItems)
  }

  for (const b of billable) updateMarkupBilling(b.id, { invoiceStatus: 'invoiced' })
  updateMarkup(markup.id, { status: 'billed', updatedAt: now }, activeEmployeeId)
  return { submitted: true }
}
```

Called identically from two places: `AddWorkModal.tsx`'s final Save step, and `MarkupPanel.tsx`'s "Submit to Production" button in the Billing tab — both funnel through this one function so there is only one grouping/de-dup implementation to maintain.

Inside `addProduction` (in the store), revenue for the created `PnLEntry` is the **sum of the line items' `extendedTotal`**, not footage × a rate — so the dollar figure always matches the billing lines exactly, never a derived approximation.

### Keeping later edits in sync (the two-way link)

Two FKs make this possible: `ProductionEntry.sourceMarkupId` (→ `FieldMarkup.id`) and `ProductionLineItem.sourceMarkupBillingId` (→ `MarkupBilling.id`). A shared helper reconciles a changed line item back into its parent entry and P&L row:

```ts
const applyLineItemToProduction = (d, productionLineItems, entryId) => {
  const siblings = productionLineItems.filter((li) => li.productionEntryId === entryId)
  const newFootage = round(sum(siblings.filter((li) => li.uom === 'LF').map((li) => li.quantity)))
  const newRevenue = round(sum(siblings.map((li) => li.extendedTotal)))
  return {
    productionLineItems,
    production: production.map((e) => e.id === entryId ? { ...e, footage: newFootage } : e),
    pnl: pnl.map((e) => e.productionEntryId === entryId ? { ...e, revenue: newRevenue } : e),
    projects: recomputeFootage(projects, production),
  }
}
```

**Editing a billing line directly** (`updateMarkupBilling`): if `quantity`/`rate`/`total` changed on a line that already has a linked `ProductionLineItem` (found via `sourceMarkupBillingId`), that line item's `quantity`/`rateSnapshot`/`extendedTotal` are overwritten to match, then rolled up via the helper above.

**Editing the markup's own `Quantity` field** (`updateMarkup`, e.g. via `WorkObjectPropertiesPanel`): this is subtler, because `FieldMarkup.quantity` only ever *seeds* a billing line's quantity once (when the line is first added) — after that they're independent records. So the cascade only follows billing lines that are **still tracking the old quantity 1:1** (`b.quantity === oldQuantity`); a line that was ever manually edited away from that value is left alone. For each still-linked line, its billing quantity/total update, and if it also has a submitted production line item, that gets updated too via the same helper.

**Deleting a markup** (`softDeleteMarkup`): hard-removes every `ProductionEntry` with `sourceMarkupId === id`, every `PnLEntry` whose `productionEntryId` was among those, every `ProductionLineItem` on those entries, and any production-linked photo blobs — then recomputes project footage.

### Design takeaway for a rebuild

If you're reimplementing this, the core invariant to preserve is: **`ProductionLineItem`/`ProductionEntry`/`PnLEntry` are a materialized, editable-in-place projection of `MarkupBilling`, keyed by explicit FKs (`sourceMarkupId`, `sourceMarkupBillingId`) — never a one-time, forever-frozen copy.** Getting this wrong (as an earlier version of this exact codebase did, before the FK was added) means a foreman can correct a quantity in the field and the P&L silently keeps the old number forever.

---

## 23. How Data Is Saved, Loaded, Exported, and Restored

### Persistence

- **Key**: `localStorage['fiberlytic:data:v1']` — the entire `AppData` object, JSON-stringified.
- **Load**: on mount, `JSON.parse` the stored value and run it through a `migrateData()` back-compat pass (fills in newly-added array fields with `[]`, promotes legacy shapes) — if storage is empty or corrupt (parse throws), silently falls back to a generated seed dataset instead of erroring.
- **Save**: a `useEffect` keyed on the whole `data` object writes it back on **every** state change, first stripping any lingering base64 `dataUrl` fields off `projectFiles` (blobs live only in IndexedDB) — write failures (quota exceeded) are swallowed as non-fatal.
- **IndexedDB**: `src/lib/fileStore.ts` — database `fiberlytic-files`, one object store `blobs`, a plain key→dataURL map (`saveBlob`/`loadBlob`/`deleteBlob`). Everything binary (photos, videos, attachments, rendered PDF pages) lives here, keyed by string ids like `mkp-<recordId>`, specifically to stay clear of localStorage's small (~5–10MB) per-origin quota.

### Export / backup / restore — the honest answer: there is no full-data backup feature

Searched broadly; what exists is narrow and domain-specific, **not** a general-purpose backup:
- A **"Reset sample data"** button — destructive, not a backup: it replaces all local data with a freshly generated seed after a confirm dialog, with no save-before-reset step.
- A P&L weekly CSV export (Dashboard).
- A Map Reading session's detections, exportable as JSON (unrelated feature).
- KMZ/KML geometry export (§10) — geometry only, not the full data model.
- An XLSX bulk-*importer* for rate-card units only (not an AppData restore mechanism).

**If you need real backup/restore/export-all for a production deployment, it does not exist today and would need to be built** — the natural shape would be a `JSON.stringify(data)` download/upload pair, since the whole state is already one serializable object; the harder part would be re-hydrating the IndexedDB blobs referenced by it.

---

## 24. Security, Permissions, and Validation

**This is the section most likely to surprise someone integrating this feature, so read carefully.**

### Two unrelated identity systems

**1. `AuthContext.tsx` — real authentication (Firebase)**

```ts
// src/App.tsx (top-level gate)
const { user, loading, setupDone } = useAuth()
if (loading) return <FullScreenSpinner />
if (!setupDone) return <SetupWizard />   // first-run: create the one admin account
if (!user) return <Login />              // email/password sign-in, Firebase Auth
```
`AuthContext` wraps Firebase's `onAuthStateChanged`/`signInWithEmailAndPassword`/`signOut`/`sendPasswordResetEmail`, with a "remember me" toggle switching between `browserLocalPersistence` and `browserSessionPersistence`. This genuinely blocks access to the entire app — including the Field Map — without a valid Firebase session. It requires the six `VITE_FIREBASE_*` env vars (§6) to be configured; if they're not, `firebaseConfigured` is false and this gate presumably can't be satisfied.

**2. `RoleContext.tsx` — a UI-only "admin vs. field view" toggle, NOT real authorization**

```ts
export type AppRole = 'admin' | 'field'
const [role, setRoleState] = useState<AppRole>(() => localStorage.getItem('fiberlytic:role') === 'field' ? 'field' : 'admin')
const setRole = (r) => { localStorage.setItem('fiberlytic:role', r); setRoleState(r) }
// isAdmin: role === 'admin'
```
This is a **plain string in `localStorage`**, toggled by a button in the app's layout ("Switch to Admin view"). There is no server check of any kind — because there is no server. Any authenticated user can flip this to `'admin'` and reload. `activeEmployeeId` (also `RoleContext`, also plain `localStorage`) is just a "who am I acting as" label with zero credential verification.

`isAdmin` **is** used to gate entire routes at the app level (`/crews`, `/employees`, `/pnl`, `/pay-stubs`, `/rate-cards`, `/equipment`, `/map-cuts`, `/map-reading`, `/photos`, `/invoicing` all require it) — **but the Field Map route itself (`/kmz/:projectId`) is a shared route, reachable by both roles.**

### Inside the Field Map feature specifically: zero role-based gating

A direct check of `KmzMap.tsx`, `MarkupPanel.tsx`, `AddWorkModal.tsx`, and `WorkObjectPropertiesPanel.tsx` confirms: **none of them reference `isAdmin` at all.** Every user, admin or field role, sees identical drawing tools, the identical Billing tab, the identical "Submit to Production" button, and the identical Delete button. The only access-control-shaped concept present is **`lockedAt`** (a per-markup lock toggle) — that's a workflow lock anyone can set/unset, not a permission.

### Validation

All validation in this feature is **client-side form validation**, not security validation — covered exhaustively in §15 (`detailsErrors`/`photosErrors`/`billingErrors`). There is no server to re-validate against, so a determined user could bypass any of it via devtools; the validation exists to guide honest field use, not to enforce trust boundaries.

### Rebuild guidance

If you're integrating this into a system that *does* have a real backend/API, you will want to:
1. Keep Firebase (or your own) auth as the actual gate.
2. **Do not** port `RoleContext` as-is if you need real authorization — it's a UI convenience, not a security boundary. Replace it with server-verified roles/claims.
3. Decide whether any Field Map actions (delete, submit-to-production, editing an invoiced line) should be permission-gated in your version — today, none are.

---

## 25. Mobile/Tablet/Desktop Behavior

Responsiveness is handled **entirely via Tailwind breakpoint classes** — there is no `window.innerWidth`/`matchMedia` branching anywhere in `KmzMap.tsx`.

### Mobile bottom nav (`lg:hidden`)

```ts
const [mobileNav, setMobileNav] = useState<'map' | 'markers' | 'forms' | 'billing' | 'settings'>('map')
```
A fixed bottom bar (only rendered below the `lg` breakpoint) with five tabs:
- **Map** — dismisses any open flyout sheet.
- **Markers** — opens a flyout grid of structure-marker buttons.
- **Forms** — currently an explicit **stub** ("QA/QC and Damage Report templates will live here").
- **Billing** — jumps straight to the first not-yet-billed markup and opens the same desktop `MarkupPanel` sidebar (there is no separate mobile-specific billing layout).
- **Settings** — toggles `mapLayer` between street/satellite directly (no panel).

A floating orange **"+Add Work" FAB** overlays the nav bar's top edge, opening the same `AddWorkModal` used on desktop.

### Touch handling

Every drag-draw and click-accumulation interaction has **fully parallel touch handlers** (not a shared abstraction) — e.g. `handleDrawMouseDown`/`handleDrawTouchStart` both call the same `drawStart()`, differing only in how they extract a `LatLng` from the event (`map.mouseEventToLatLng(e.nativeEvent)` vs. a manual `touchToLatLng(e.touches[0])` helper). A native tap listener is registered directly on the Leaflet container (rather than relying on Leaflet's built-in tap→click synthesis) specifically because that synthesis "can miss taps or fire too late" on phones — tracking `touchstart`/`touchend` coordinates directly and rejecting any >15px movement as a pan rather than a tap.

### Camera vs. file picker

Already covered in §16 — the distinguishing mechanism is simply the `capture="environment"` HTML attribute on a dynamically-created file input, which mobile browsers honor (open rear camera directly) and desktop browsers silently ignore (falls back to a normal file picker).

### Desktop-only elements

A handful of toolbar buttons use `hidden sm:flex` to appear only at wider breakpoints; the overall page shell adjusts its outer negative margins by breakpoint (`-mx-4 -my-6 lg:-mx-6`).

---

## 26. Developer Integration Guide

### Step 1 — Decide your data-layer strategy

The cleanest integration path is to treat `DataContext.tsx`'s CRUD surface as the contract, and reimplement its *body* against your own backend while keeping every component's calls to `useData()` unchanged. The methods you need, at minimum, for Field Map parity:

```ts
addMarkup(m): string
updateMarkup(id, patch, actor?): void
deleteMarkup(id): void
softDeleteMarkup(id, actor?): void
addMarkupPhoto(p, actor?): string
deleteMarkupPhoto(id, actor?): void
addMarkupVideo(v): string
deleteMarkupVideo(id): void
addMarkupBilling(b, actor?): string
updateMarkupBilling(id, patch): void
deleteMarkupBilling(id, actor?): void
addMarkupInspection(i): string
updateMarkupInspection(id, patch): void
deleteMarkupInspection(id): void
addMarkupAttachment(a): string
deleteMarkupAttachment(id): void
addFieldMapOverlay(o): string
updateFieldMapOverlay(id, patch): void
deleteFieldMapOverlay(id): void
addAerialLashFiberRun(run): string
updateAerialLashFiberRun(id, patch): void
deleteAerialLashFiberRun(id): void
toggleFavoriteUnitCode(code): void
addProduction(entry, lineItems?): string   // if you want the production/P&L link (§22)
```

If you're building on a real backend, this is also the moment to add server-side authorization (§24) — this codebase has none to carry over.

### Step 2 — Port the type definitions

Copy §4's interfaces into your own `types.ts`. They have no external dependencies beyond each other.

### Step 3 — Port the blob store

`src/lib/fileStore.ts` is 47 lines, zero dependencies, framework-agnostic IndexedDB wrapper — copy it verbatim, or replace it with uploads to your own object storage (in which case, change `MarkupPhoto`/`MarkupVideo`/`MarkupAttachment`'s blob-reference convention from `idb:<id>` to real URLs).

### Step 4 — Install dependencies

```bash
npm install leaflet pdfjs-dist tesseract.js jszip polygon-clipping
npm install -D @types/leaflet
```
Skip `mapbox-gl` and `@supabase/supabase-js` — both are dead weight for this feature (§5).

### Step 5 — Port the components and lib files, in this order

1. `src/lib/engineeringSymbols.ts`, `src/lib/workObjectTypes.ts`, `src/lib/markupMeta.ts`, `src/lib/constructionTools.ts` — pure data/catalog files, no dependencies on the rest.
2. `src/lib/markupLayer.ts`, `src/lib/geometryOps.ts`, `src/lib/snap.ts`, `src/lib/georeference.ts` — geometry/rendering logic.
3. `src/lib/workObjectCallout.ts`, `src/lib/workObjectPanelPosition.ts`, `src/lib/markupDelete.ts`.
4. `src/lib/productionFromMarkup.ts`, `src/lib/laborCost.ts` — only if you want production/P&L linkage (§22); skip if you just want redlining/photos/billing without the downstream cost roll-up.
5. `src/components/SymbolIcon.tsx`, `PhotoImg.tsx`, `MarkupQuickActions.tsx`, `MarkupDeleteConfirm.tsx`, `AddWorkDiscardConfirm.tsx`, `AddWorkTypeGrid.tsx`.
6. `src/components/WorkObjectPropertiesPanel.tsx`, `MarkupPanel.tsx`, `AddWorkModal.tsx`, `GeoreferencePanel.tsx`, `LayerManagerPanel.tsx`, `FieldMapToolbar.tsx`.
7. `src/pages/KmzMap.tsx` last — it's the integration point for everything above.

### Step 6 — Adapt the shared UI primitives

`Modal`, `Field`/`Input`/`Select`/`Textarea`/`Button`, `Card`/`CardHeader`/`CardBody` are simple Tailwind components with no exotic dependencies — either port them or swap in your own design system's equivalents; none of the Field Map logic depends on their specific implementation, only their prop shapes.

### Step 7 — Decide what to leave behind

- **PDF Print Mode** (`PdfPrintMode.tsx`, `markupToPdfSvg.tsx`) — only needed if you want redlining directly on a static PDF page in addition to the live map. Skip it for a map-only integration.
- **Map Cuts** (§20) — entirely separate; only port it if you also need the "split one big plan sheet into field-print pages" workflow. It shares no code with the map.
- **Aerial Lash Fiber runs** (`AerialLashRunPanel.tsx`, `PoleFormModal.tsx`) — a fairly specific sub-feature (per-pole tracking along an aerial strand); skip if not needed.
- **Everything under `src/features/printkmz/` except `pdf.ts` and `ocr.ts`'s `runOcrWithBoxes`** — dead code (§9c/§10), don't port it.
- **`syncQueue.ts`** — an inert placeholder; only port it if you're intentionally building toward the same "queue locally, flush on `online` event" pattern for your own future backend. Otherwise, just call your real API directly from the CRUD methods and skip this layer.

### Step 8 — Verify end-to-end after integration

A practical smoke test, mirroring this feature's own real behavior:
1. Draw a line, pick a Work Type, fill in required Details/Photos/GPS, add a billing line, Save — confirm a `FieldMarkup` + `MarkupBilling` + (if wired) `ProductionEntry`/`PnLEntry` all exist.
2. Reload the page — confirm the markup, its photos, and its billing survive (localStorage/IndexedDB or your backend, whichever you wired).
3. Edit the billing line's quantity after submission — confirm the linked production/P&L figure updates in place, not duplicates.
4. Delete the Work Object — confirm it disappears from the map but its audit history (if you kept that concept) survives, and any invoiced line blocks the delete.
5. Test on a touch device — confirm drawing gestures and the camera-capture button both work.
